docs.text: Hello! I'm an Assistant Professor in the Department of Aerospace Engineering at Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay. I'm interested in understanding, modeling and simulating the mechanics and thermodynamics of solids, with a special focus on the physics of various kinds of defects in crystalline solids. You will find more information about my research and teaching activities at IIT Bombay, and about the people in my research group, in the following pages. Here is a brief summary of my academic background: 2001-2006: B.Tech/M.Tech Dual Degree (Aerospace Engineering) - IIT Madras . 2006-2012: Ph.D. (Aerospace Engineering) - Indian Institute of Science . 2012 (May - July): Visiting Researcher - University of Exeter . 2012-2013: Post-doctoral researcher - Ecole Centrale de Nantes . 2014-2017: Post-doctoral researcher - California Institute of Technology . 2018-Present: Assistant Professor (Aerospace Engineering) - IIT Bombay . Outside academic research, I enjoy listening to Carnatic music (the classical music of south India), and occassionally play it on the fretless electric guitar.
docs.title: Home

docs.text: Here are additional details of my current and past research activities. A new iterative scheme to solve the Peierls-Nabarro model The Peierls-Nabarro model is one of the earliest, and still widely used, examples of a multiscale model that aims at predicting the core structure of dislocations in crystalline materials. The key idea behind the Peierls-Nabarro model is that the energy of a dislocated crystal can be thought of as the sum of the bulk elastic energy and the surface energy associated with the slip plane bounded by the dislocation. Despite its simplicity and shortcomings, the Peierls-Nabarro model is one of the most successful continuum model of dislocation cores. Dissociation of edge and screw dislocations on {111} planes in Aluminium. In a recent work , I proposed a new iterative scheme to solve a generalized version, due to Schoeck, of the Peierls-Nabarro model. Unlike existing techniques, the proposed method exploits certain properties of the Hilbert transform to reduces the solution of the Peierls-Nabarro model to a simple fixed point iteration scheme. An application of the new iterative scheme to study the dissociated core structure of both edge and screw dislocations on {111} planes in Aluminium is illustrated above. Check out the preprint for more details. Nano-void growth mechanism in Magnesium As part of my post-doctoral research at Caltech, funded by the MEDE program, I studied spallation in Magnesium (Mg) using the quasicontinuum method . The interest in Mg is primarily due to its promise as a lightweight alternative to conventional metals for various engineering applications. Mg naturally occurs in the hexagonal close packed (HCP) crystal structure. The HCP structure has low symmetry in comparison to other crystal structures like the face centered cubic (FCC) structure, and this has specific implications on how plastic deformation is accommodated at the atomic scale. Unlike FCC materials where slip systems dominate, the role of twinning is crucial for understanding the plastic response of HCP materials. Plasticity around nano-voids in Magnesium To understand the atomistic basis of spall, we (I, along with Mauricio Ponga , Kaushik Bhattacharya and Michael Ortiz ) studied the time evolution of nano-voids in single crystal Mg subject to tensile strain loads. This study led to the important conclusion that the absence of any significant interactions between dislocation activity on the basal plane, involving basal slip and prismatic loop formation, and $\langle c+a \rangle$ dislocations that accommodate deformation perpendicular to the basal plane, plays a crucial role in the observed lower spall strength of Mg. Check out our paper for more details

docs.title: Contact

docs.text: Research Interests I'm primarily interested in multiscale modeling of the mechanical response of materials. A proper understanding of the mechanics and thermodynamics of materials requires a careful analysis of phenomena occurring across a wide span of length and time scales, from the sub-atomic to the continuum, and their scale spanning interactions. A faithful modeling of the material behavior thus necessitates the use of very different models at different scales: quantum mechanics at the sub-atomic scale, classical mechanics at the atomic scale, statistical mechanics at the mesoscale, and effective continuum models at the macro scale. I draw upon tools from mathematics, physics, computation and data science, to explore some of these multiscale aspects of the mechanics and thermodynamics of materials. I'm also interested in certain aspects of wave propagation in solids . More details about my current and previous research activities can be found on this page, and on my research blog . Preprints A. A proper understanding of the mechanics and thermodynamics of materials requires a careful analysis of phenomena occurring across a wide span of length and time scales, from the sub-atomic to the continuum, and their scale spanning interactions. A faithful modeling of the material behavior thus necessitates the use of very different models at different scales: quantum mechanics at the sub-atomic scale, classical mechanics at the atomic scale, statistical mechanics at the mesoscale, and effective continuum models at the macro scale. I draw upon tools from mathematics, physics, computation and data science, to explore some of these multiscale aspects of the mechanics and thermodynamics of materials. docs.text: Details of the courses I teach at IIT Bombay can be found here. Note that this page will be continuously updated to reflect the latest course content. The lecture notes for the courses are still evolving. As a working solution , I have hosted some of the course material in separate webpages. PDF versions of the final lecture notes will be uploaded here as and when the notes reach some degree of maturity. AE 337/AE 731 - Multiscale Modeling of Materials Fall 2018 This is a new graduate level elective dealing with various aspects of modeling the mechanical behavior of crystalline materials across various length and time scales, from atomistic to the continuum. Topics covered include basic ideas in Quantum, Classical, Statistical and Continuum Mechanics, along with relevant numerical techniques. Assignments for this course will be uploaded on the IITB Moodle. AE 486/AE 639 - Continuum Mechanics Course material Introduction: slides . Mathematical prerequisites : This webpage contains lecture notes providing an elementary introduction to essential topics in tensor algebra and analysis to get started with continuum mechanics. Note that this webpage is still under construction: it will be revised and updated over the course of this semester. Continuum Mechanics : This webpage is meant to provide a basic introduction to certain key ideas in continuum mechanics. Please note that this webpage is still under construction: it will be revised and updated over the course of this semester. Fall 2019 This time around, AE 639 will be taught using the flipped classroom model. All lectures will be video lectures, links for which will be uploaded on the IITB moodle. The actual lectures in the classroom will be discussion based. The primary reference for this course are the video lectures. The TAs for this course will be Lotti Rushi and Divyesh Mistry. Note that the classroom for this course is the Aerospace Annex Lecture Hall. Spring 2019 The TAs for this course this year are Sneha Cheryala and Lalit Bhola. The classes will be held in the Aerospace Annex Lecture Hall. Spring 2018 This is a senior undergraduate/beginning graduate elective in Continuum Mechanics. The course TA for this year is Ashish Kumar Bodla. Please refer to the most recent offering of the course for slides and/or lecture notes.
